California
Fair Political
Practices Commlsslon
April 
■9, 199■
」ohn R. Shaw′  City Attorney
City of San 」uan Capistrano
32400 Paseo Adelanto
San 」uan Capistrano, CA 92675
Re:  Your Request for AdViCe
Our File No. I-9■
-139
Dear Mr. Shaw:
You are seeking advice on behalf of city of san Juan
capistrano Planning Commissioner Dennis Haehn with regard to his
duties and respons iui t it ies under the conf I ict-of - interest
provisions of tne Political Reform Act (the rraq1rr1.1 As city
ittor.,"y and Mr. Haehn's authorized representative we provide you
with advice pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy encl-osed) . 
Because
your question does not reference a specific pending governmental
a"cisio.,, the advice provided in thii letter is inioimal only.2
The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your
letter of March l, f Sgf., teLephone conversations with you on March
12, Lggl and tvith Mr. Tom Tornlinson of the city's planning
department on March '7, LggL and a letter dated March 12, 1991 fron
t',tr. Tomlinson in response to th j.s of f ice's request f or additional
inforrnation.
OUESTTON
Forthcoming decisions before the city's planning comnission
concern a proposed 60-1ot subdivision whose closest boundary to
Mr. Haen's residence is approxirnately 1.5oo feet. 
May Mr' Haen
participate in the planning comrnission's decision concerning the
proposed subdivision tentative map?
・  GoVernment COde SectiOns 8■000-910■5.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
■ndicated.  Comm■ss■on
regulat■ons appear at 2 Californ■a Code of Regulations Section
18000′  et seq.  All references tO regulations are to Title 2′
Div■s■on 6 0f the Californ■a Code of Regulations.
2    1nfOrmal asSiStance doeS not provide the requestor with the
immunity proVided by an opinion or fOrmal Written adviCe.
(SeCtion 83114′  RegulatiOn 18329(C)。
)
428 J Street,Suite 800 ● P.0.Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804・0807 ● (916)322‐ 5660
/


File No. I -91- 13 9
Page 2
CONCLUS TON
Mr. Haehn may participate in the planning commission's
decisions regarding the proposed subdivision provided the
decisions wi-l-1 not have a foreseeabLe material financial effect on
his property interests which is distinguishabLe fron the effect on
the public generally. 
FACTS
The city of San Juan capistrano planning comrnission is
scheduled to hold its first public hearing on a proposed tentative
subdivision map for a 6o-Iot proposed subdivj,sion. Planning
commissioner Dennj.s Haehn's residence is located approximately
1,50o feet from the nearest proposed subdivision boundary li.ne.
ANALYS I S
rf a public official 
knows or has reason to know he or she
has a financial interest in a governmental- decision, the official
is prohibited from making, participating in, or using his or her
official 
position to j.nf luence the decision- (Section 87100.) A
public of f i-cia1 is f inanc j.alIy interested in a decision when it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decj.sion will have a naterial
financial effect, distinguishable fron its effect on the public
generaJ.ty, on the of f j.cial, a member of officiaL's 
i.mmediate
family, or on any rea] property in which the official 
has a direct
or indirect interest ',vorth one thousand (Sf,000) or more-
(Section 87103 (b) . )
.As a planning commissioner, Mr. Haehn is a public officiaL
(Section 82048). and we assume that his i.nterest in hj's property
is worth one thousand dolLars or more. He must therefore
disqual j.f y himself from particj.pating in decisions concerning the
proposed subdivision which wiIl. have a reasonably foreseeable
matLrial financial effect on him or hj-s real property interests
that j.s distinguishable from the effect on the public generally'
(Section 87103 (b) . )
Foreseeable Material financial Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it wiLl occur. Certainty is not
required, but an effect that is merely a possibility is.not
reasonably foreseeable' (Downev cares v- Downev Communitv
Development com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983; In re Thorne! (1975)
1 FPPa-ops. 198.) The Act, however, does seek to prevent even the
appearan-e of a possi.ble conflict of interest. 
(witt v' Morroe'
(t917 ) 7 O Ca l. ApP. 3d 817 , 823 , )


File No. I-91-139
Page 3
commissj.on Regulation L8702.3 (copy enclosed) assists in the
determination whether the foreseeable effect of a decision is
material as to real property in which an official 
has an interest.
You have indicated that ur. Haehn's property is located
approxirnately 1,500 feet from the site of the proposed
subdivision. For projects qrith a boundary of between 300 and
2,500 feet from an official's 
property, the effect of the decision
will be considered rnaterial if it will have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars (S10,000) or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official 
has an interest; or
(B) will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,ooo ore more per 12 nonth period.
Regulation L8702.3 (a) (3) (A) and (B).
r,Jhether the effect of the decision is positive or negative is of
no consequence under the Act. 
(Younq Advice Letter, A-89-L49,
copy enclosed. )
should the effect on his property of the decisions regarding
the proposed subdivision faII below the financial thresholds
specifiea in the regulation, Mr. Haehn's participation in the
decisions would be permitted. we have not been provided suffi-
cient information to enable. or otherwise support, a conclusion
whether or not the financial thresholds wilI be met. In providing
advice the Commission does not act as a finder of fact,3 and woul'd
not evaluate the accuracy of whatever facts that are offered. But
without such facts, our advice srith respect to the application of
Ehe requlation to Mr. Haehn's participation can only be of a
gener.i nature.4 we do $/ish to draw your attention to Regulation
iezoz.:(a) . This regulation provides sone factors to consider in
determininq the maqnitude of the financial irnpact on Mr- Haehn's
property. 5'
I As such, the advice provided in thj's letter is applicable only
to the exlent that al.1 material and correct facts have been
presented. (In re oqlesbv, (1975) l- FPPC ops. 77, copy enclosed. )
4 S." Moe Advice Letter, A-90-580, copy enclosed.
5 such factors include the proxinity of the properties and the
magnitude of the proposed project in relationship to Mr- Haehn's
pr6perty, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
t., tf," proposed subdivision wiIl affect the development potential
or i-ncome producing potential of Mr. Haehn's property, and whether
it is reasonably foreseeable that the proposed subdivision will
result in a change of the character of the neighborhood including,
but not Iimited to, the effect on traffic, 
view, privacy,
intensity of use, noi,se IeveIs, air emissions or sirnilar traits.


FiIe No. I -91- 13 9
Page 4
The I'Public ceneralLyr' Exception
If you conclude that the specified financia] impact and other
requisites as provided for in Regulation la1o2.3 (a) (3) (A) and (B)
are present, Mr. Haehn would have a disqualifying conflict.
Nevertheless, he could stiII 
be pernitted to participate in
decisions concerning the proposed subdivision if the effect of the
decision on his otherwise disqualifying interests $ras not
distinguishable fron the effect on the public Aenerally. (section
87103. )
The financiaL effect of the planning commission's decision on
Mr. Haehn's property is distinguishable fron the effect on the
public generally unless the decision's affect is substantially the
sane as it is on all rnembers of the public or a significant
segment of the public, (Regulation 18703; Jorqensen Advice Letter,
No. A-90-o17,' In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1,'In re O$ren (1976)
2 FPPC ops. 77, copies enclosed. ) The "public AeneralIy" is
comprised of the entire jurisdiction of the agency in question.
(In re Leqan, supra. ) If a disqualifying conflict is otherwise
present, you must deternine, based on your circumstances, whether
the "public generallyI exception would app]y to pernit Mr. Haehn's
partici.pation in the subdivision decisions. Those facts
indicating how, and whether, the proPosed subdivision decision
wiII impact aII, or a significant segment, of the city's residents
would required assessment. o
I hope this letter has provided you with some of the guidance
you requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, or wish to supplernent your advice request with additional
information, please contact me at (915) 322-59OL.
S incerely,
sH: JSR : ken
Enclosures
6 Th" factors previously discussed in Regulation 18702.3(d) !.rouId
be relevant in any consideration of whether the public generally
exception would apply. A group that is large in nunber and
heterogeneous in quality can constitute a significant seg:ment of
the public for the purposes of the public generally exception.
(In re Ferraro (19?8) 4 FPPC ops. 62.) To assist you in
eval-uating the "public AeneraIJ.y" exception, I have enclosed
copies of other Cornmission advice letters which have addressed the
issue. Enclosed are: EegE Advice Letter, No. A-89-243; Scher
Advice Letter. No. A-88-479; Flvnn Advice Letter, No. I-88-430.
counse I ,
Division
